items.title: The Impact of Large Language Models on Open-source Innovation: Evidence from GitHub Copilot
items.authors: Doron Yeverechyahu, Raveesh Mayya, Gal Oestreicher-Singer
items.summary:
arXiv:2409.08379v4 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are reshaping knowledge work, yet their impact on voluntary, self-guided open innovation forums (contributors choose tasks without managerial direction) may differ fundamentally from effects observed in organizational settings. We study this question in open-source software development, where individuals' contributions collectively drive innovation at a community level. Unlike product innovation, where typologies for classifying innovation are well established, knowledge work in open-source settings calls for a distinction grounded in the cognitive demand a task places on the contributor. Burgeoning literature distinguishes substantive contributions, which require creative problem formulation to introduce new functionality, from incremental contributions, which draw on comprehension of existing code to maintain and refine it. We exploit a natural experiment around GitHub Copilot's launch in October 2021, where Copilot supported languages like Python while not supporting R for business reasons, creating an exogenous partition between otherwise comparable ecosystems. Using three complementary identification strategies and two classification approaches, we find that Copilot availability increases open-source contributions by 28 to 40 percent. The increase in incremental contributions is significantly larger than the increase in substantive contributions across all specifications. This disparity is more pronounced in projects with higher activity levels and widens following a model upgrade: LLMs function more effectively when existing context helps define the problem and constrain solutions, tilting collaborative innovation toward exploitation of established codebases rather than exploration of new functionality. This paper provides a rare instance of causal field evidence on LLM effects, given the speed at which GenAI has exploded across the knowledge economy.
items.claude_summary: A natural experiment around GitHub Copilot's October 2021 launch — where Copilot supported Python but not R for business reasons — provides causal evidence that LLM coding assistance increases open-source contributions by 28 to 40 percent. The growth is uneven: incremental contributions (maintaining and refining existing code) rise significantly more than substantive contributions (introducing genuinely new functionality). This gap widens in high-activity projects and after model upgrades, suggesting LLMs are most effective when rich existing context defines the problem and constrains solutions. The implication is that AI tilts collaborative innovation toward exploitation of established codebases rather than exploration of new territory — a structural shift in how open knowledge communities produce and extend ideas.
items.centre_angle: This study shows that LLM assistance does not lift all contributions equally — it disproportionately amplifies incremental, comprehension-based work while leaving substantive, exploratory contributions relatively unchanged. From CAMO's perspective on AI and incentives, this mirrors a concern raised in our work on generative AI and professional effort: when AI compresses the cost of adequate-quality output, agents may rationally redirect effort away from harder, higher-value tasks. In open-source settings, where contributors self-select tasks without managerial oversight, the reallocation toward exploitation over exploration may be especially difficult to correct because there is no principal able to reprice the incentive for frontier contributions.

matched_camo.title: Better Technology, Worse Motivation: GenAI and the Mediocrity Trap
matched_camo.reason: Both studies find that AI assistance flattens ambition toward lower-effort, adequate-quality output rather than sustaining the effort required for genuinely novel work.

items.key_takeaway: AI coding tools grow the volume of open-source contributions substantially, but the gains concentrate in routine maintenance rather than original invention — a pattern managers and platform designers should account for when forecasting AI's net effect on innovation pipelines.
items.visual_concept: A single large forking tree diagram rendered as a clean architectural blueprint — white lines on a deep navy background — centered in the frame at medium scale. The trunk and lower branches are thick and densely annotated, representing the established codebase; the upper branches taper and thin sharply, conveying the relative scarcity of new exploratory growth. A diffuse pool of cool white light falls from above onto the mid-section of the tree, where the branching is most active, while the sparse upper tips recede into shadow. The surrounding space is empty blueprint grid, quiet and uncluttered, so the contrast between the dense lower structure and the sparse upper canopy reads immediately.
red_flags: Working paper (arXiv preprint, not yet peer-reviewed in a journal); findings should be treated as preliminary until formal publication.
Study is limited to open-source software (Python vs R ecosystems), so generalizability to proprietary organizational settings or non-coding knowledge work requires caution.
Classification of contributions as 'substantive' vs 'incremental' relies on automated approaches whose validity is not fully assessable from the abstract alone.

items.title: AI in the Enterprise: How People Use M365 Copilot Chat
items.authors: Scott Counts, Yan Chen, Jing Dong, Himanshu Sharma, Andrey Zaikin, Rui Hu, Alperen Kok, Gorkem Ozer Yilmaz, Siddharth Suri, Kiran Tomlinson, Sonia Jaffe, Will Wang
items.summary:
arXiv:2605.23958v1 Announce Type: cross 
Abstract: M365 Copilot is used every week by millions of people across more than a million companies around the world as part of their workflows. Uniquely positioned in the AI landscape given its near-exclusive use for work purposes, M365 Copilot can offer a clear picture of how people use AI for work and where that usage may expand next. This paper characterizes that usage through direct classification of user interactions with M365 Copilot Chat. Based on an anonymized and privacy-preserving analysis of a sample of approximately 5.5 million sessions, we combine a learned classification of user intent with a classification of O*NET work activities done with M365 Copilot Chat. We find that M365 Copilot is emerging as an everyday assistant for knowledge work: writing dominates, but users also rely on it for information retrieval, analysis, decision making and strategizing, and evaluating and diagnosing programs and systems, among others. Information seeking tasks remain common, but time trends suggest a relative shift away from ``chat as search'' and toward content and communication-related work. Comparisons across occupational groupings and to work done in the labor market further show that usage is broad but uneven, where the relative share of work done with M365 Copilot Chat cuts across jobs in some cases and is occupation-specific in others. Areas of relative underrepresentation in the labor market suggest the next frontier for enterprise AI adoption.
items.claude_summary: Drawing on anonymized data from roughly 5.5 million M365 Copilot Chat sessions, this study maps what knowledge workers actually do with enterprise AI. Writing tasks dominate, but usage also spans information retrieval, analysis, decision support, and diagnosing systems. A notable time trend shows users shifting away from treating the tool as a search engine and toward content creation and communication tasks. Usage is broad across occupational groups but uneven — some tasks are occupation-specific, others cut across job types. Where Copilot is underrepresented relative to actual labor market work provides a signal, the authors argue, of where enterprise AI will expand next.
items.centre_angle: This study offers one of the largest behavioral maps of enterprise AI use to date, showing that adoption has moved past curiosity and into daily workflow — but that penetration remains uneven across task types and occupations. From CAMO's perspective on AI adoption, the uneven distribution matters as much as the aggregate: organizations that understand which tasks and roles are underserved by current tools are better positioned to close the gap between nominal deployment and genuine organizational impact. CAMO's Deloitte-HKU AI Adoption Index similarly found that adoption is nearly universal yet shallow in most firms, and this study's task-level data helps explain why — the tools are present, but uptake concentrates in a narrow band of activities.

matched_camo.title: Deloitte-HKU AI Adoption Index 2026: The Paradox of Promise and Performance
matched_camo.reason: Both studies document the gap between broad nominal AI adoption and shallow or uneven actual use, making them natural complements for understanding why enterprise AI underdelivers on ROI.

matched_camo.title: Compressing Coordination with AI at Decathlon China
matched_camo.reason: The Decathlon case illustrates how AI optimizes individual tasks but leaves coordination bottlenecks untouched — a structural parallel to this study's finding that Copilot usage clusters in discrete task categories rather than reshaping end-to-end workflows.

items.key_takeaway: Enterprise AI is becoming a genuine daily work tool for knowledge workers, but usage concentrates in writing and information tasks — the gaps in coverage show where the next wave of adoption will, and must, occur.
items.visual_concept: A single open-plan office desk, centered slightly left of frame at mid-ground scale, with a monitor displaying a minimal document interface — no brand markings. The desk is surrounded by a sparse, softly blurred open-plan environment that recedes into near-darkness, so no competing elements draw the eye. A single overhead light pools directly onto the desk surface and screen, creating a cone of cool white illumination that falls away into warm shadow at the edges of the frame. The lighting palette is cool-white at the focal point grading to amber at the periphery, suggesting concentration amid a larger, quieter organizational space. The mood is workday-functional rather than futuristic — grounded, routine, slightly solitary.
red_flags: Not peer-reviewed — arXiv preprint, posted May 2026; findings have not yet undergone formal peer review.
Authors are affiliated with Microsoft, which develops and sells M365 Copilot; findings should be read with that conflict of interest in mind.
Data is observational and drawn from a proprietary, non-public sample; replication by independent researchers is not currently possible.
The excerpt does not detail how 'sessions' are defined or how the classification model was validated, limiting assessment of measurement reliability.

items.title: Generative AI as a Design Variable: An Evidence-Centered Framework for Principled Governance in STEM Assessment
items.authors: Yizhu Gao, Zhongzhou Chen, Min Li, Xiaoming Zhai
items.summary:
arXiv:2605.24837v1 Announce Type: new 
Abstract: Generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI) presents a governance challenge for STEM assessment. Unrestricted GenAI access enables task outsourcing that undermines the validity of traditional assessments; blanket prohibitions are difficult to enforce, may push use underground, and do little to prepare students for workplaces where GenAI-supported workflows are increasingly common. This paper addresses this dilemma by proposing a framework grounded in Evidence-Centered Design (ECD) that treats GenAI as a design variable within the assessment argument rather than an external threat to it. The framework analyzes how GenAI reshapes the student model, evidence model, and task model, and uses this analysis to articulate three principled governance stances. Restrict is warranted when GenAI would contaminate the inferential link between student work products and targeted unaided proficiency. Scaffold is warranted when bounded GenAI support can support peripheral demands without revealing the target construct, preserving inferential interpretability. Require is warranted when the target construct is disciplinary AI interaction competency and tasks can be designed to elicit process artifacts, including prompts, critiques, and revisions, that make student reasoning observable, scorable, and distinguishable from AI-generated output. This framework specifies when to restrict, scaffold, or require GenAI use in STEM assessment. We present two task designs deployed in an introductory physics course and demonstrate that disciplinary AI interaction competencies are observable in student response artifacts and can be scored using defensible rubrics grounded in student data and expert knowledge. By situating GenAI governance within validity arguments, the framework offers actionable guidance for preserving learning integrity while supporting authentic preparation for AI-enabled professional environments.
items.claude_summary: How should institutions govern generative AI in STEM assessments — ban it, permit it, or require it? This paper proposes a framework rooted in Evidence-Centered Design that treats GenAI as a built-in design variable rather than an external threat. The core argument: the right governance stance depends on what skill is being measured. Restrict GenAI when it would short-circuit the inference between student output and target ability. Allow bounded use when it handles peripheral demands without revealing the core skill. Require it when the target competency is disciplinary AI interaction itself — prompting, critiquing, revising — and when process artifacts can be captured and scored. The framework is demonstrated in an introductory physics course, where AI-interaction competencies proved observable and scorable.
items.centre_angle: This paper argues that the binary choice between banning and permitting GenAI in assessment is a false dilemma, and that governance should instead follow the logic of what a valid inference requires. From CAMO's perspective on AI & Jobs, the deeper implication is organizational: as workplaces increasingly embed GenAI in professional workflows, educational institutions face the same challenge firms do — how to design evaluation systems that remain meaningful when AI assistance is ubiquitous. CAMO's research on the hidden competence penalties associated with AI use at work ('Research: The Hidden Penalty of Using AI at Work') suggests that how AI use is signaled and interpreted matters enormously; a generation trained under inconsistent AI-governance norms will enter firms where those ambiguities have real career consequences.

matched_camo.title: Research: The Hidden Penalty of Using AI at Work
matched_camo.reason: Both the paper and this article grapple with the social and institutional norms surrounding visible AI use — one in educational assessment, the other in professional evaluation — and both find that how AI assistance is perceived shapes behavior and outcomes in ways institutions have not yet resolved.

items.key_takeaway: Governing GenAI in assessment is not about prohibition or permission but about specifying precisely which competency is being measured and designing tasks where human reasoning remains visible regardless of AI involvement.
items.visual_concept: A single examination desk, centered in the frame and shot from a slight high angle, holds one open notebook with a partially completed handwritten problem set alongside a tablet displaying a GenAI chat interface. The desk sits in an otherwise spare, softly lit classroom — rows of empty chairs recede toward a vanishing point behind the desk, pulling the eye back to it. Light falls in a cool, diffuse wash from a high window at the upper left, casting a soft shadow from the notebook toward the lower right, distinguishing the handwritten work from the screen's faint blue glow. The surrounding environment is deliberately uncluttered: pale concrete walls, no decorative elements, so the tension between the two work surfaces — pen and screen — carries the full visual weight.
red_flags: Preprint only — posted to arXiv, not yet peer-reviewed; findings from a single introductory physics course limit generalizability.
Empirical demonstration is narrow in scope; the rubric-scoring claims would benefit from independent replication across disciplines and institutions.

Primary audience and contribution are in educational assessment research; relevance to business management or policy audiences requires an explicit bridging argument not fully present in the paper itself.

items.title: Generative AI impacts on intra-urban inequality and skill premium in Beijing
items.authors: Xiliu He, Haoxiang Zhao, Mingyi Ma, Edward Wen Chuan Lai, Koei Enomoto, Anni Hu, Jiatong Li, Lingyun Chu, Yuan Lai
items.summary:
arXiv:2605.25505v1 Announce Type: cross 
Abstract: Generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) is the first automation wave to reach high-cognitive tasks at scale, yet its effects on intra-urban inequality remain largely unknown. Using 5 million job postings from Beijing (2018--2024), we construct a neighborhood-level GenAI Exposure Index by aggregating task-level assessments from five leading large language models. We examine the spatial, structural and causal mechanisms of this shock. We find that GenAI exposure is highly concentrated in the city's core districts, deepening the intra-urban AI divide. Since 2023, high-exposure neighborhoods have experienced wage stagnation even as they continue to attract high-skilled workers -- a "high-skill trap." This wage penalty is driven by task de-skilling and intensified labor-market crowding. A difference-in-differences design centered on ChatGPT's release supports a causal interpretation. These findings challenge the prevailing theory of skill-biased technological change and provide a basis for inclusive AI governance in global technology hubs.
items.claude_summary: Using 5 million job postings from Beijing (2018–2024), this study builds a neighborhood-level index of GenAI exposure and finds that the technology's impact is spatially uneven and paradoxical. Districts with the highest GenAI exposure have seen wage growth stall since 2023, even as they continue drawing highly skilled workers — a dynamic the authors call a 'high-skill trap.' The mechanism is task de-skilling and labor-market crowding: GenAI erodes the cognitive premium that once justified high wages in knowledge-intensive roles. A difference-in-differences design using ChatGPT's release as a natural experiment supports a causal reading. The findings challenge standard skill-biased technological change theory and suggest that GenAI may deepen rather than bridge intra-urban economic divides.
items.centre_angle: This study argues that GenAI's arrival in high-cognitive labor markets is producing a 'high-skill trap': workers with the credentials to use AI continue to cluster in exposed districts, but the wage premium for their skills is being eroded by task de-skilling and crowding. From CAMO's perspective on AI and jobs, this spatial evidence from Beijing gives empirical texture to a concern that is often argued theoretically — that AI can simultaneously attract talent and compress the returns to that talent. CAMO's working paper 'Toward a Bad Job Economy' models a structurally similar dynamic: AI lowers the cost of satisfactory performance, weakening the economic case for sustaining high-effort, high-wage roles, even when those roles generate more total surplus.

matched_camo.title: Toward a Bad Job Economy: AI Adoption, Agency Costs, and Job Design
matched_camo.reason: Both works identify a mechanism by which AI depresses wages in skilled roles — the Beijing paper through labor-market crowding and task de-skilling, the CAMO paper through the rising incentive cost of sustaining high effort — pointing to a shared structural concern about AI and wage polarization.

items.key_takeaway: GenAI is stalling wages in Beijing's most knowledge-intensive neighborhoods by de-skilling cognitive tasks and flooding those districts with workers whose premium is now harder to justify — a warning that AI exposure and economic benefit do not automatically coincide.
items.visual_concept: A single elevated walkway or pedestrian bridge — concrete, utilitarian, shot from slightly below and off-center at the golden-ratio left — stretching toward a dense cluster of glass office towers in the middle distance, with the towers rendered in cool, desaturated blue-grey tones. The bridge itself is the focal subject, positioned in the lower foreground and leading the eye via a single-point perspective toward the corporate core. The surrounding city falls into soft focus and shadow on either side, keeping visual weight on the path and the towers it leads to. Lighting is flat overcast daylight — diffuse, no strong shadows — giving the scene an ambiguous, neither-promising-nor-threatening mood. The overall palette is cool grey and muted steel-blue, with a faint warmth only at the horizon where the towers meet the sky, suggesting aspiration that is structurally constrained.
red_flags: Preprint only — posted on arXiv, not yet peer-reviewed; findings should be treated as preliminary.
Exposure index is constructed from LLM task-level assessments, which introduces model-dependent measurement assumptions that may not be fully validated.
Study is limited to Beijing; generalizability to other cities or labor markets requires caution.
The difference-in-differences design uses ChatGPT's release as an exogenous shock, but the plausibility of the parallel-trends assumption is not assessable from the abstract alone.

items.title: PAIRED: A Process-Anchored Framework for Transparent Reporting of AI Contributions in Scientific Research
items.summary:
arXiv:2605.24325v1 Announce Type: new 
Abstract: The rapid integration of generative AI into scientific research has exposed a critical gap in academic disclosure practice. Existing frameworks for reporting AI contributions are uniformly output-oriented -- they document what AI produced, not how the research unfolded. As a result, researchers who wish to report their AI collaboration honestly lack the tools to do so: no current framework can distinguish between a researcher who originated a research direction and one who adopted a direction proposed by AI, or between a researcher who critically evaluated AI-generated alternatives and one who accepted AI output without independent assessment. This gap is not a matter of compliance detail; it is a failure to capture the cognitive dynamics that determine what kind of intellectual contribution a paper actually represents.
  We propose PAIRED -- Process-Anchored Interaction Reporting for AI-Enabled Discovery -- a dual-facing framework that addresses this gap through four design principles: process orientation, which takes the decision point rather than the research product as the fundamental unit of documentation; dual-facing output, which derives a structured publisher disclosure from a prospective author log without double work; decision-point granularity, which operates between session-level coarseness and message-level impracticality; and artifact-triggered logging, which provides an auditable rule against selective omission. We demonstrate PAIRED through worked examples, discuss its limitations openly, and propose a model-assisted adoption pathway that embeds the framework's logging discipline directly into AI research platforms.
items.claude_summary: Scientific publishing has no standard way to document how AI was used during research—only what it produced. This paper identifies that gap as substantive, not cosmetic: current disclosure norms cannot distinguish a researcher who independently conceived a study from one who adopted an AI-suggested direction, or one who critically evaluated AI outputs from one who accepted them uncritically. The proposed PAIRED framework reorients disclosure around decision points in the research process, generates both an author log and a publisher-facing summary from the same inputs, and embeds logging triggers into the workflow to prevent selective omission. The authors demonstrate the framework through worked examples and propose embedding it directly into AI research platforms.
items.centre_angle: This paper argues that AI disclosure in research has stalled at the level of output attribution—documenting what AI wrote or generated—while the more consequential question of cognitive agency remains unaddressed. From CAMO's perspective on AI adoption, the implication is organizational as much as scholarly: the same ambiguity that undermines academic accountability also surfaces inside firms deploying AI in knowledge work, where it is rarely clear whether a decision was originated by a human or proposed by a system. CAMO's work on the hidden competence penalties employees face when AI use is visible (see 'Research: The Hidden Penalty of Using AI at Work') suggests that without credible process-level disclosure, organizations will continue to conflate AI-assisted output with unassisted capability—with real consequences for evaluation, trust, and incentive design.

matched_camo.title: Research: The Hidden Penalty of Using AI at Work
matched_camo.reason: Both papers grapple with how organizations interpret and evaluate AI-assisted work: PAIRED addresses the absence of process-level disclosure that would let evaluators distinguish genuine intellectual contribution from AI delegation, which is precisely the attribution problem that drives the competence penalty documented in the CAMO study.

items.key_takeaway: Without process-level records of how AI was used—not just what it produced—neither research institutions nor organizations can reliably assess the intellectual contribution behind AI-assisted work.
items.visual_concept: A single open research notebook centered in the lower-middle of the frame, its pages filled with handwritten annotations and printed text, positioned on a plain matte desk with minimal surroundings. A narrow shaft of cool, directional daylight falls directly onto the notebook from the upper left, leaving the edges of the frame in soft shadow and pulling the eye to the document as the sole focal subject. The lighting palette is cool grey-white with a faint warm tone on the paper itself, suggesting careful scrutiny rather than urgency. The scene is spare—no figures, no devices visible—conveying the idea of documentation and process record rather than technology.
red_flags: Not peer-reviewed: preprint posted to arXiv, no indication of journal acceptance or external review.
Proposal paper only: PAIRED is a conceptual framework demonstrated through worked examples; no empirical validation of adoption, compliance rates, or real-world effectiveness is reported.
Single author with no disclosed institutional affiliation visible in the excerpt; potential for limited peer scrutiny.
Relevance to CAMO's core management and organizational audience is indirect; the framework targets academic publishing norms rather than firm-level AI governance.

items.title: Authority Signals in Claude AI Health Citations: A Descriptive Analysis Using the Authority Signals Framework
items.authors: Erin T. Jacques (York College, CUNY), Erela Datuowei (Teachers College, Columbia University), Elizabeth Quaye (York College, CUNY), Corey H. Basch (William Paterson University), Arijit Chatterjee (York College, CUNY), Juanita Davis (York College, CUNY)
items.summary:
arXiv:2605.23921v1 Announce Type: new 
Abstract: This study seeks to determine the authority signals used by Anthropic's Claude AI in its presentation of sources when answering consumer health questions. While there exists a great deal of discourse around the quality of health citations that LLMs produce, there is limited information on the integrity of the sources the citations originate from, and to what extent the sources are, from what health professionals would consider, credible sources. This descriptive cross-sectional study used data from HealthSearchQA, which contains 3,172 consumer health questions curated by Google Research. After exclusions, a final dataset of 3,075 questions yielding 10,038 citations was analyzed. The Authority Signals Framework (Jacques et al., 2026) was applied to examine 10 authority signals across four domains for a disproportionate stratified sample of 542 sources. Established institutional sources accounted for 97.8% of all citations (n = 9,818). Medical Institutions were the most frequently cited organization type (36.5%), followed by Government Resources (31.6%) and Professional Associations (28.4%). Commercial Health Information comprised 2.2% (n = 220). The top 10 organizations accounted for 57.8% of all citations, with Mayo Clinic alone representing 24.7%. Among commercial sources in the focused sample, 86.4% displayed medical review statements, 82.5% used schema markup, and 71.8% had comprehensive content, while traditional institutional sources appeared in Claude's citations with or without these same markers. As Anthropic positions Claude for HIPAA-ready healthcare applications, these findings establish a baseline for Claude's citation behavior and demonstrate the utility of the Authority Signals Framework as a tool for ongoing, cross-platform evaluation of AI-mediated health information.
items.claude_summary: When consumers ask Claude health questions, where do its citations actually come from — and how credible are those sources? Analyzing 10,038 citations generated from 3,075 health questions, this study finds that 97.8% of Claude's citations point to established institutional sources: medical institutions (36.5%), government resources (31.6%), and professional associations (28.4%). The top 10 organizations account for 57.8% of all citations, with Mayo Clinic alone representing nearly one in four. Commercial health sources, while rare, display more overt authority signals — medical review statements, schema markup — than traditional institutions, which appear in citations regardless of such markers. The study establishes a baseline for evaluating Claude's citation behavior as Anthropic pursues HIPAA-ready healthcare deployments.
items.centre_angle: This study documents a structural concentration in how Claude selects and presents health information sources — a small set of institutional names dominates nearly 60% of all citations, while commercial sources appear to benefit from SEO-style authority signals that may influence retrieval. From CAMO's perspective on AI Algorithms & Data, the finding raises a quiet but important question about whether LLM citation behavior reflects genuine epistemic quality or proxies for institutional brand visibility and technical markup. As AI systems move into high-stakes decision contexts like clinical health information, understanding the mechanics of source selection — and its potential biases — becomes a governance concern as much as a technical one.
items.key_takeaway: Claude's health citations are heavily concentrated in a handful of trusted institutions, but commercial sources appear to earn inclusion through SEO-style signals rather than traditional credibility markers — a distinction that matters as AI enters clinical settings.
items.visual_concept: A single open reference book centered in the frame, slightly off-center to the left at golden-ratio position, its pages fanning open and casting a long shadow to the right across a clean white surface. Behind the book, a sparse vertical hierarchy of faintly labeled rectangular blocks — like a simple org chart or citation stack — recedes toward a single vanishing point at the top-center of the image, drawing the eye from the book upward. The environment is minimal: a flat, uncluttered surface with no competing objects. Lighting is cool and directional, a soft daylight from the upper left that pools on the book's open pages, suggesting clinical clarity rather than warmth. Palette is white, cool grey, and a single muted institutional blue on the topmost block in the hierarchy.
red_flags: Preprint — arXiv submission, not yet peer-reviewed; findings should be treated as preliminary.
Descriptive cross-sectional design limits causal inference; the study documents patterns but cannot establish why Claude selects sources it does.
Sample is drawn from a single LLM (Claude) at a single point in time; generalizability to other models or future Claude versions is limited.
The Authority Signals Framework cited (Jacques et al., 2026) appears to be the authors' own instrument, creating a potential circularity in validation.

items.title: From Replacement to Orchestration: A Socio-Technical Architecture for Agentic AI in Corporate R&D
items.authors: Haithem Boussaid, Marc Heemskerk, Jimmy Sim\'eon, Adam Breen, Merouane Debbah
items.summary:
arXiv:2605.24580v1 Announce Type: new 
Abstract: Purpose: Corporate R&D faces a persistent productivity paradox: rising investment and expanding scientific knowledge have not translated into proportional innovation output. In pharmaceuticals this is captured as Eroom's Law; analogous patterns appear across engineering, materials science, and healthcare. The core cause is not insufficient tools but cognitive saturation: researchers spend an increasing share of their effort on coordination, documentation, and data governance -- hidden work that displaces high-value hypothesis formation, interpretation, and strategic synthesis. Design/Methodology/Approach: The paper uses a Design Science Research (DSR) methodology. The artifact is the HARMONY operating model. Evidence is triangulated from four semi-structured expert interviews with senior R&D leaders across industrial, healthcare, and academic settings; a foresight scenario analysis projecting four plausible 2040 R&D futures; and pattern matching with documented agentic R&D deployments. Two non-negotiable design requirements guide the architecture: cognitive-load redistribution (DR1) and bounded autonomy with alignment (DR2). Findings: We propose HARMONY -- Hybrid Agentic Research Model for Organisational New Yield -- a four-pillar socio-technical architecture comprising ResOps (Industrialized Execution), the Control Tower (Strategic Visibility and Drift Detection), the Ethics Fabric (Bounded Autonomy by Design), and the Talent Studio (Sciencepreneur Capability). The model introduces the Sciencepreneur as the central human archetype in agentic R&D, and Orchestration Leverage as a candidate productivity metric suited to human-agent hybrid systems.
items.enrichment_error: enrichment failed after 2 attempts: Unterminated string starting at: line 30 column 5 (char 4187)

items.title: Microsoft’s Path to Adopting and Scaling AI Across its Sales Organization
items.summary: In early 2024, six months after the highly anticipated launch of Microsoft Copilot across the 62,000-person Microsoft Customer and Partner Solutions (MCAPS) organization—one of the world’s largest sales organizations—the initial excitement had not yet materialized into widespread adoption and transformation. But, two years after initiating their AI transformation journey, the organization’s daily active usage of AI tools had reached over 60% and monthly active usage over 98%, significantly altering how sales professionals approached their work. The path to adoption had required Microsoft to evolve its approach based on early deployment insights. Harvard Business School Associate Professors Iav Bojinov and Shunyuan Zhang join Brian Kenny to discuss the case, “Microsoft Customer and Partner Solutions: The Deployment of Copilot and Agents.” They explore the company’s journey to successfully mobilizing AI adoption within the sales process, the challenges it faces integrating autonomous sales agents, what it takes to get thousands of employees to fundamentally change how they work.
items.claude_summary: Two years into deploying Microsoft Copilot across its 62,000-person sales organization, Microsoft reached daily active AI usage above 60% and monthly active usage above 98%. The path was not straightforward: six months after launch, adoption had stalled and the company had to rethink its deployment strategy based on early evidence. A Harvard Business School case study examines how Microsoft evolved its approach, what it took to shift entrenched working habits at scale, and what challenges remain as the organization moves toward integrating autonomous sales agents. The excerpt is drawn from a podcast discussion of the case and does not detail specific mechanisms or quantitative causal findings.
items.centre_angle: This case documents how a large enterprise moved from near-zero habitual AI use to near-universal monthly adoption, and what managerial interventions were required to close that gap. From CAMO's perspective on AI adoption, the Microsoft experience illustrates a recurring pattern: technical deployment is the easy part; the organizational and behavioral work of changing how thousands of employees actually operate is where most initiatives stall. Our Deloitte-HKU AI Adoption Index finds that the dominant barriers facing executives today are similarly organizational — siloed departments, cultural resistance, and the absence of visible quick wins — rather than anything technical. Microsoft's two-year arc is a large-scale test of what it takes to move past those barriers.

matched_camo.reason: The Microsoft case directly mirrors the index's central finding that AI adoption is widespread in intent but shallow in measurable impact, with organizational and executional factors — not technology — as the binding constraint.
matched_camo.title: Deloitte-HKU AI Adoption Index 2026: The Paradox of Promise and Performance

matched_camo.reason: The stalled early adoption at Microsoft is consistent with CAMO's finding that social penalties for visible AI use create friction that access, training, and infrastructure alone cannot resolve.
matched_camo.title: Research: The Hidden Penalty of Using AI at Work

items.key_takeaway: Getting to 98% monthly AI usage across a 62,000-person sales force took Microsoft two years of adaptive management, not just a product rollout — the organizational work dwarfed the technical work.
items.visual_concept: A floor plan of a large open-plan office rendered as a heat map, with color saturation representing AI tool usage spreading unevenly across departments over time — dense in some clusters, sparse in others — illustrating diffusion as an organizational topology problem rather than a switch being flipped.
red_flags: Source is a podcast episode summarizing an HBS teaching case, not a peer-reviewed study — causal claims about what drove adoption should be treated as practitioner narrative, not empirical evidence.
Quantitative figures (60% daily active usage, 98% monthly) are reported without definition of the denominator, measurement window, or methodology — numbers may be self-reported by Microsoft.
Potential conflict of interest: Microsoft is both the subject and a major technology partner; HBS case studies are sometimes developed with significant company cooperation, which can shape framing.
The RSS excerpt is thin; specific mechanisms, interventions, and findings are not detailed, limiting how much the article can be represented with confidence.

items.title: EXOTIC: An Exact, Optimistic, Tree-Based Algorithm for Min-Max Optimization
items.authors: Chinmay Maheshwari, Chinmay Pimpalkhare, Debasish Chatterjee
items.summary:
arXiv:2508.12479v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: Min-max optimization arises in many domains such as game theory, adversarial machine learning, etc. For these problems, gradient-based methods are well understood and enjoy strong guarantees. However, in the absence of convexity or concavity, existing approaches study convergence to an approximate saddle point or first-order stationary points, which may be arbitrarily far from global optima. In this work, we present an algorithmic framework for computing the global minimax value in convex--non-concave and non-convex--concave min-max optimization. For convex--non-concave min-max problems, we use a reformulation that transforms the problem into a non-concave--convex max-min optimization problem with suitably defined feasible sets and objective function. This reformulation can be viewed as an extension of Sion's minimax theorem to the convex--non-concave setting. We then introduce EXOTIC -- an Exact, Optimistic, Tree-based algorithm for solving the reformulated max-min problem. EXOTIC combines an iterative convex optimization solver for the inner minimization with an optimistic hierarchical tree search for the outer maximization, inspired by StroquOOL~\cite{bartlett2019simple}. Unlike StroquOOL, which assumes stochastic zero-mean noisy evaluations, EXOTIC handles deterministic, biased, and budget-dependent evaluation errors arising from finite-time solutions of the inner convex subproblems. We establish an upper bound on its optimality gap. The same framework also applies to non-convex--concave min-max optimization. Empirically, EXOTIC outperforms gradient-based methods on popular benchmarks from the literature. Finally, we demonstrate the utility of EXOTIC by computing security strategies in multi-player games with three or more players -- a computationally challenging task that, to our knowledge, no prior method solves exactly.
items.claude_summary: A new algorithm called EXOTIC tackles a class of optimization problems — min-max problems — that appear throughout game theory and adversarial machine learning, but where standard gradient-based methods can get stuck far from the true best solution. The core challenge: when the problem lacks certain mathematical properties (convexity or concavity), existing approaches settle for approximate solutions that may be nowhere near the global optimum. EXOTIC combines a tree-search strategy for the outer problem with a convex solver for the inner problem, and can handle the messy, biased errors that arise in real computation. The authors show it finds global solutions and demonstrate its value in computing security strategies in multi-player games — a problem prior methods could not solve exactly.
items.centre_angle: This paper contributes a theoretically grounded algorithm for global min-max optimization, addressing a gap where gradient-based methods reliably fail in non-convex settings. From CAMO's perspective on the statistical and algorithmic foundations of trustworthy AI-driven decisions, the relevance lies in the downstream: robust adversarial machine learning and multi-agent game equilibria depend on exactly this kind of global-solution guarantee rather than approximate convergence. The connection is more foundational than applied — the work is unlikely to reach managers directly, but the class of problems it solves underpins reliability claims in deployed AI systems.

matched_camo.title: Asymptotic Theory for IV-Based Reinforcement Learning with Potential Endogeneity
matched_camo.reason: Both papers develop algorithmic frameworks with formal theoretical guarantees for sequential decision-making problems modeled as dynamic optimization processes, addressing failure modes of standard methods.

items.key_takeaway: Standard gradient methods can settle far from the true optimum in adversarial and game-theoretic settings; EXOTIC offers a tree-based alternative with global guarantees, which matters for any AI system whose reliability rests on finding true equilibria rather than approximate ones.
items.visual_concept: A single bare decision tree rendered in cool steel-blue, centered in the frame at medium scale, its branches extending symmetrically outward against a plain off-white background. A vanishing point placed directly behind the tree's root node draws the eye inward along converging perspective lines on a flat geometric floor plane, suggesting depth and systematic search. The environment is stripped of clutter — no labels, no figures — letting the tree's architecture carry the meaning. Lighting is soft and diffuse, with a gentle gradient from pale grey at the edges to near-white at the focal root, reinforcing the sense of convergence toward a solution.
red_flags: Not peer-reviewed: arXiv preprint only, no indication of journal acceptance.
Highly technical algorithmic paper with limited direct relevance to organizational or managerial AI topics — audience fit for CAMO's primary readership is weak.
Empirical benchmarks described but underlying datasets and competitor implementations not independently verified from the excerpt alone.

items.title: A Need for Nuance: The Economist’s Andrew Palmer
items.authors:
Sam Ransbotham.

Me, Myself, and AI is a podcast produced by MIT Sloan Management Review and hosted by Sam Ransbotham. It is engineered by David Lishansky and produced by Allison Ryder.

Sam Ransbotham is a professor in the information systems department at the Carroll School of Management at Boston College, as well as guest editor for MIT Sloan Management Review’s Artificial Intelligence and Business Strategy Big Ideas initiative.
items.summary: On today’s episode of the Me, Myself, and AI podcast, Andrew Palmer, senior editor at The Economist, describes how organizations can experiment with generative AI while balancing speed, quality, and risk. At his own organization, Andrew and others test artificial intelligence with human oversight to develop editing and publishing efficiencies. As the host of The […]
items.claude_summary: Andrew Palmer, senior editor at The Economist, discusses how news organizations can experiment with generative AI while managing the tension between speed, quality, and editorial risk. Speaking on the MIT Sloan Management Review podcast, Palmer draws on his own organization's experience of testing AI tools under human oversight to find efficiencies in editing and publishing workflows. The conversation centers on the need for organizational nuance—avoiding both uncritical enthusiasm and reflexive skepticism—when integrating AI into professional knowledge work. The excerpt is thin on specific findings or data; the discussion appears to be practitioner-level reflection rather than empirical research.
items.centre_angle: This podcast episode argues that responsible AI adoption in knowledge-intensive organizations requires deliberate experimentation under human oversight, rather than wholesale deployment or blanket avoidance. From CAMO's perspective on AI adoption, the real organizational challenge is not access to the technology but the governance structures—who tests, who oversees, and how quality standards are maintained—that determine whether AI integration adds genuine value. Our Deloitte-HKU AI Adoption Index finds that the dominant barriers to AI adoption are organizational and executional rather than technical, a pattern consistent with Palmer's emphasis on nuance over speed.

matched_camo.reason: Both the episode and the index identify organizational execution—not technical capability—as the central obstacle to realizing value from AI, and both emphasize that measured, structured experimentation is preferable to undifferentiated rollout.
matched_camo.title: Deloitte-HKU AI Adoption Index 2026: The Paradox of Promise and Performance

items.key_takeaway: Effective AI adoption in professional knowledge work depends less on the technology itself and more on building organizational structures that allow controlled experimentation without sacrificing quality or accountability.
items.visual_concept: A single broadsheet page or printed proof sheet centered in the lower-middle third of the frame, lying flat on a plain light-grey desk surface. The page is rendered in crisp black-and-white editorial type, with one short paragraph visibly struck through in red pencil—suggesting revision and human judgment at work. A single overhead light source falls directly onto the page, creating a soft pool of warm white light that fades into cool grey shadow at the frame edges, keeping the surrounding environment bare and uncluttered. The composition uses the contrast between the illuminated page and the receding grey surface as the sole compositional device, drawing the eye immediately to the act of editing. Palette: cool grey background, warm neutral paper tone, single accent of editorial red.
red_flags: Podcast episode, not peer-reviewed research; findings are practitioner reflections, not empirical results.
RSS excerpt is very thin—summary relies on limited disclosed content; core arguments cannot be fully verified without listening to the full episode.
No quantitative evidence or organizational data cited in the available excerpt.
